channel.title: Appl. Phys. Rev.
channel.description: Filtered papers from Appl. Phys. Rev.

item.title: Filtered cathodic vacuum arc: Unlocking the future of multifunctional coatings through hybrid deposition techniques
item.description: The advent of filtered cathodic vacuum arc (FCVA) technology has revolutionized the deposition of high-performance multifunctional coatings by combining dense microstructures with minimal defect formation. Unlike conventional physical vapor deposition and chemical vapor deposition processes, FCVA operates effectively at low temperatures while simultaneously addressing two long-standing challenges: macroparticle contamination and high-thermal stress on substrates. These advantages enable its successful application across diverse industrial domains, including semiconductor device fabrication, aerospace engineering, biomedical implants, and flexible electronic systems. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the fundamental principles, technological evolution, and material systems enabled by FCVA. Attention is given to plasma filtration architectures and their influence on ion transport, macroparticle suppression, and their relationship to film growth. Recent developments in substrate biasing strategies, reactive plasma control, and real-time plasma diagnostics are discussed as key approaches for regulating stress, microstructure, and coating uniformity. In addition, the integration of FCVA with other plasma-based deposition techniques is examined as a route to extending compositional flexibility and functional performance. By enabling the design of tailored multifunctional coatings, FCVA is poised to accelerate the deployment of next-generation technologies ranging from temperature-sensitive electronic devices to lightweight aerospace structures and biocompatible medical systems. The paper concludes with a forward-looking discussion on scalability, cost considerations, and sustainable processing strategies, outlining the conditions under which FCVA may remain competitive within the broader landscape of advanced coating technologies.

item.title: Exceptionally anisotropic thermal expansion in flat-band zero-dimensional hybrid perovskites
item.description: Achieving highly anisotropic thermal strain in solids remains a fundamental challenge, as conventional semiconductors typically exhibit thermal expansion anisotropy ratios, Δα/αave (defined as the relative difference between linear expansion coefficients α along two crystallographic directions), below 1. Here, we report an unprecedented degree of thermal expansion anisotropy in hierarchical zero-dimensional hybrid perovskites, where colossal positive thermal expansion ( α up to +149 × 10−6 K−1) and strong negative thermal expansion ( α down to −94 × 10−6 K−1) coexist along different crystallographic axes, resulting in an anisotropy ratio reaching 5.6. This extreme anisotropy originates from orientation-dependent highly anharmonic phonon dynamics driven by hydrogen-bonding-dominated symmetry-broken local microforces and globally flat electronic bands that enforce electron localization. Distinct from purely covalent or ionic interactions, these complex microforces substantially amplify the thermal anisotropy within the hybrid perovskite framework. Between 120 and 560 K, the accumulated anisotropic strain yields a breathing-mode lattice response and triggers a cascaded structural transition. These results unravel the microscopic underlying mechanism responsible for the giant thermal anisotropy in hierarchical hybrid solids. They also guide designs of thermal strain materials for high-precision microelectromechanical systems, tunable optoelectronics, and strain-adaptive functional devices.
